concerns.notes: Fix IS present and correct in ci.yml:105-108: `while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac; IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id"); done` — newline-agnostic (the `|| [ -n "$id" ]` keeps the final newline-less line) and comment/blank tolerant. So fix_addresses_finding holds. BUT the test does NOT guard the actual config: test_supply_chain.py:98 runs against a HARDCODED COPY (_PARSE_LOOP, lines 26-36), not the ci.yml content. I reverted the REAL ci.yml allowlist loop back to the buggy `IGNORES=""; while read -r id; do [ -n "$id" ] && IGNORES="$IGNORES --ignore-vuln $id"; done` form and ALL 7 supply-chain tests stayed GREEN. The behavioral test proves the embedded logic is correct (and even asserts the buggy variant drops the id), but it cannot catch a regression of ci.yml itself — unlike #130/#132/#147 which read ci.yml directly. Non-discriminating against the real config => CONCERN.

Fix IS present in ci.yml: IGNORES is now a bash ARRAY (line 103 `IGNORES=()`, line 107 `IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")`) expanded quoted as `"${IGNORES[@]}"` (line 110), so allowlist entries cannot word-split or glob-expand, and the `case ... \#*) continue` skips comments. fix_addresses_finding holds. BUT same discrimination gap as #131: test_supply_chain.py:126 (test_allowlist_ids_do_not_glob_expand) runs against the hardcoded _PARSE_LOOP copy, not ci.yml. Reverting the REAL ci.yml to the unquoted-string `$IGNORES` form left all 7 tests GREEN. The behavioral test confirms the embedded array logic prevents glob expansion (GHSA-* not expanded against cwd), but provides no guard tied to the workflow file. Non-discriminating against config regression => CONCERN.

The finding's requested fix (tighten `arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18` to e.g. `>=17,<18`, and de-duplicate the langgraph>=1.2,<2 bound across persistence+orchestration extras) was NOT applied. pyproject.toml is unchanged in the entire pass-2 (git diff 1389242..HEAD -- pyproject.toml is EMPTY); arize-phoenix is still `>=7.0,<18` (pyproject.toml:55) and langgraph is still duplicated (lines 60 and 66). The team instead addressed only the reproducibility half via the lockfile (#132), which pins arize-phoenix==17.6.0 for CI installs. That mitigates resolved-version drift for the audited artifact, but a downstream library consumer installing `assay-engine[observability]` from PyPI (no lock) still resolves against the over-wide >=7.0,<18 range — exactly the surface the finding flagged. No test guards the pyproject range or the langgraph dedup (the lockfile test only asserts the LOCK is exactly pinned, test_supply_chain.py:68, which would pass for ANY range). Fix does not address the finding as written and is non-discriminating => CONCERN.

The finding requested THREE supply-chain monitoring controls; only ONE was implemented. (1) SBOM emission: DONE — ci.yml:111-124 emits a CycloneDX SBOM (`pip-audit ... --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json`) and uploads it as an artifact; test_supply_chain.py:91 (test_sbom_is_emitted) discriminates (removing `cyclonedx-json` from ci.yml FAILED the test; restored -> PASS). (2) `.github/dependabot.yml` for automated transitive-update PRs: ABSENT (file does not exist). (3) Automated license/copyleft allowlist gate: ABSENT (no pip-licenses / license check anywhere in ci.yml; only a comment at line 115 mentions license monitoring). So 2 of the 3 requested controls are missing — the finding is only partially addressed. The implemented SBOM piece is genuinely fixed and tested, no ADR/scope regression, but the fix is incomplete vs the finding => CONCERN.

The fix (the new threaded test test_concurrent_get_checkpointer_runs_setup_once, tests/test_persistence.py:544-562) addresses the described GAP in form — it launches 8 concurrent get_checkpointer() calls on a Barrier — but it is NON-DISCRIMINATING, exactly the defect the finding warned about. I removed the bootstrap serialization (replaced `with _conn_init_lock(conn_str):` at checkpoint.py:236 with contextlib.nullcontext()) and the test still PASSED 5/5 runs (setup ran once every time). The race window is too narrow: FakeSaver.setup (test line 402) does no work, so under the GIL each thread completes the cache-empty->open-pool->setup->cache-write section before the next is scheduled. The fix's own instruction ('optionally a small sleep inside FakeSaver.setup to widen the window... Verify it is discriminating by confirming it fails if the lock is removed') was NOT implemented. I proved the lock IS load-bearing only after widening the window: with the lock removed AND time.sleep(0.05) inside setup, an equivalent test FAILS with 'setup ran 8 times'. So the committed test cannot demonstrate the _conn_init_lock/_init_lock matters — a regression that drops the lock would ship green, which is the precise complaint #143 raised. Recommend adding a small sleep in the fake setup to make it discriminating. (No regression/scope issue; this is purely a weak-test concern.)

Fix itself is correct: docs/METHODOLOGY.md:131-132 and docs/GOVERNANCE.md:58-60 step 3 both changed from flat 'RFC-3161 timestamped' to 'on-box HMAC by default ... an RFC-3161 TSA is pluggable for third-party non-repudiation', matching the shipped LocalHmacAuthority (preregistration.py:130; no RFC-3161 client in src/). fix_addresses_finding=true. BUT the GUARD is only half-discriminating. The METHODOLOGY half discriminates: reverting METHODOLOGY.md to stale 'RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation' made test_preregistration_timestamp_wording_matches_shipped_default FAIL at test_docs_drift.py:52 (no 'HMAC' in METHODOLOGY). However, reverting ONLY the GOVERNANCE §2 step-3 body text back to the stale 'RFC-3161 timestamped against a trusted timestamp authority' left the test GREEN — because the file-level substring checks `'HMAC' in text` / `'pluggable' in text` are still satisfied by unrelated honest-scope notes elsewhere in GOVERNANCE.md (the §2 implementation-status note L76-103). So a regression that re-introduces the exact GOVERNANCE over-claim the finding cited would ship with green CI. The GOVERNANCE half of the finding is not genuinely guarded. No scope/regression issue (docs-only).
